checks.a_analysis: The HTML output follows the same organizational structure as the source PDF. The document is divided into vendor-specific sections (Broadcom, Sophos, Trellix, CrowdStrike, Cisco) in the same order as the PDF. Within each section, the content flows logically, with headings, descriptive text, and tables appearing in positions consistent with the original document's intent. The transition from the multi-page PDF format to a single-page responsive HTML layout is handled well without compromising the meaning or relationship of content blocks.

The numerous complex tables across all 19 pages of the source document have been faithfully converted into HTML tables. The column headers (e.g., 'ITEM #', 'SKU', 'COST', 'YR 4', 'YR 5') remain visually distinct from the body rows. Multi-column cost tables, such as the CrowdStrike 'Attachment A. Cost Proposal Form' on PDF page 16 and the Cisco table on PDF page 19, are correctly rendered with all year-based pricing columns aligned and readable. Even tables that were split across pages in the PDF are rendered as cohesive units or clearly related segments in the HTML.

The source document consists almost entirely of text and structured data in tables. There are no significant meaningful images, logos, or diagrams in the source that are missing from the HTML output. The visual content consists of table borders and formatting, which is correctly represented in the HTML structure.

The HTML output does not contain any visually duplicated body content. Each vendor's price list and associated terms appear once in the body of the document. The 'Professional Services' sections are unique to their respective vendor contexts (e.g., Broadcom on PDF page 4, SHI Trellix on PDF page 11) and are not repeated blocks of identical content. The presence of headings in the Table of Contents that link to the same headings in the body is standard accessible design and does not constitute duplication.
